original_abstract:
This article provides a critical assessment of the theological framework popularized by Keith Ward, specifically focusing on his interpretation of a Christian reality. Within a general reformational theological perspective, I suggest that Ward utilizes panentheistic criteria to bridge the gap between materialist and spiritual understandings of the world. By examining the structural parallels in his work, the paper argues that Ward adopts a "soft panentheism"—a bipolar model of God that attempts to reconcile divine transcendence with a scientifically informed immanence. 

The analysis explores why panentheism has gained significant traction among contemporary Christians and explains the intellectual appeal of a bipolar approach to the divine. Finally, this article offers a series of recommendations for those who may find the allure of generic Christian panentheism a tempting substitute for the traditional, classical view of God.

Die doel van hierdie artikel is om die algemene benadering tot ’n Christelike interpretasie van die werklikheid, soos algemeen toegepas deur Keith Ward, te evalueer. Dit word voorgestel dat hy panenteïstiese kriteria toepas in sy pogings om teologiese akkommodasie van ’n materiële en geestelike interpretasie van die werklikheid te steun. Hierdie evaluering volg ’n algemene reformatoriese teologiese perspektief. Voorstelle word gemaak om te verduidelik waarom panenteïsme so ’n impak op sommige Christene het dat hulle ’n bipolêre benadering tot God verkies. Verder word aanbevelings gemaak vir diegene wat ’n Christelike generiese panenteïsme in verskillende vorme aanloklik genoeg vind om dit sodoende as ’n alternatiewe interpretasie vir die tradisionele siening van God te oorweeg.
humanized_abstract:
In this paper I want to investigate the theolo-gy of a Christian reality as formulated by the influential Anglican theologian Keith Ward. Starting from a critical general reformational theological standpoint, I will argue that Ward has used criteria of a panentheistic nature in order to accommodate what is basically a dualistic (material/spiritual) view of the world. Upon closer investigation, it appears that he has operated with a form of what I shall call "soft panentheism," namely a version of a bipolar concept of God which, by trying to preserve divine transcendence in an age of evolution and high science, ends up by attempting to accommodate the findings of the last fifty years of scientific study of the world within a general religious framework.

How do we explain the popularity of panentheism among modern Christians? Why does the bipolar model of the divine seem so appealing? This essay provides some recommendations for those tempted by the allure of generic Christian panentheism and who wish to avoid the allures of this replacement system in favor of the greater richness of the classical theism described in the Westminster Catechism.

The aim of this article is to assess the general approach to a Christian understanding of reality, as exemplified by the work of Keith Ward, and to consider whether he, in his attempts to provide a theologically adequate accommodation of materialism and dualism, uses panentheistic criteria. The evaluation is carried out from a general Reformed theological perspective. Suggestions are made to account for panentheism's influential power on some Christians leading them to opt for a bipolar concept of God. Recommendations are also made for those who find a form of Christian generic panentheism sufficiently attractive so as to consider it an alternative understanding of God as opposed to the traditional understanding.
